title: Universal Gates, K-Maps & Don't Care Conditions

content.value: Implementing any circuit with one gate type

Because NAND and NOR are universal, any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates or only NOR gates. This matters in real fabrication because manufacturing a single gate type simplifies the chip design.

AND using NAND:  A.B = ((A.B)')'  -> two NANDs
NOT using NAND:  A' = (A.A)'      -> tie both inputs together
OR using NAND:   A+B = (A'.B')'   -> three NANDs (DeMorgan)

A K-map is a grid arrangement of a truth table where adjacent cells differ by exactly one bit (Gray code ordering). Grouping adjacent 1s into the largest possible power-of-2 rectangles (1, 2, 4, 8 cells) lets you read off a minimized SOP expression directly, replacing tedious algebraic simplification.

items: 4-variable map: 16 cells, arranged with Gray-code row/column headers
Groups must be rectangular and sized as powers of 2 (1,2,4,8...)
Cells can wrap around the edges of the map

content.value: F(A,B,C,D) = sum of minterms (0,1,2,5,8,9,10) is plotted on a 4-variable K-map. Grouping the four corner cells (0,2,8,10) gives the term B'D'. Grouping (0,1,8,9) gives B'C'. Grouping (1,5,9) is not a valid power-of-2 group alone, so it pairs with another adjacent 1 to form a group of 2.

Sometimes certain input combinations never occur in practice (e.g. invalid BCD codes 10-15). These cells are marked 'X' (don't care) on the K-map. You may treat an X as either 0 or 1, whichever helps form a larger group - this often produces a much simpler circuit.
On a K-map, only use don't-care cells when they help enlarge a group that already contains at least one required 1. Never form a group made entirely of don't-cares.
Each group of 1s becomes one product term in the simplified SOP expression - only the variables that stay constant across the whole group appear in that term.

quiz.question: In a K-map, adjacent cells should differ by how many bits?
Why are don't-care cells useful?
options: They can be set to 0 or 1 to help form larger, simpler groups
They must always be treated as 1
